evidence: Docstring asks whether infinitely many n have all exponents distinct in the 'factorisation into distinct primes' of n(n+1).
Docstring: 'It is likely that there are infinitely many primes p such that 8p^2 - 1 is also prime.'
Conditional theorem concludes distinct exponents in the prime factorization of n(n+1) via Set.InjOn factorization.
Docstring asks for a graph with aleph_2 vertices and 'chromatic number aleph_2' whose small subgraphs have chromatic number at most aleph_0.
The question is phrased entirely in terms of aleph_2, aleph_1 and aleph_0 cardinalities of vertex sets and chromatic numbers.
Erdos-Hajnal [ErHa68b] result: graphs of chromatic number aleph_1 with aleph_k vertices whose smaller subgraphs have chromatic number at most aleph_0.
f(n) is maximal such that every point x in an n-point planar set has at least f(n) points 'equidistant from x'.
Strong form asks f(n) < n^(c/log log n) for the same maximal-equidistant-points function f.
f_k(n) is defined as 'the maximum possible chromatic number of a graph with n vertices which contains no K_k'.
The graphs range over K_k-free graphs (G.CliqueFree k), i.e. graphs containing no k-clique.
Docstring derives the f_4 bound from 'the lower bound R(4,m) >> m^3/(log m)^4 of Mattheus and Verstraete [MaVe23]'.
Rodl's theorem: if G has chromatic number >= f(k) then G contains a triangle-free subgraph with chromatic number >= k.
The conclusion is a 'triangle-free subgraph' (H.CliqueFree 3) of large chromatic number.
Asks whether the product over r disjoint long intervals of consecutive integers 'is not a perfect power' (IsPower).

Docstring: 'Erdos and Selfridge [ErSe75] proved that the product of consecutive integers is never a power' (Theorem 1 of ErSe75), which is exactly the Erdős-Selfridge theorem item (fr/de sitelinks describe the 1975 Diophantine theorem).
States that a product of at least two consecutive integers is never a perfect power (IsPower).
Asks whether only finitely many pairs of products of consecutive integers 'have the same prime factors' (primeFactors equality).
Erdos was unable to prove 'there must exist a prime between n_1 and n_2' when the two products share prime factors.
Concerns integers between consecutive primes p_r < n < p_{r+1} with all prime factors below the gap p_{r+1} - p_r.
The counted n are exactly (p_{r+1}-p_r)-smooth: 'all of whose prime factors are < p_{r+1} - p_r'.
Erdos showed the set of r with at least one such n between p_r and p_{r+1} (prime factors below the gap) has density 0.
k and l are defined as 'the 2-adic valuation' and 'the 3-adic valuation' (padicValNat) of n(n+1), and the limsup question is about 2^k 3^l.
